summary:
The correct answer is A because it reflects an actual gap between early expectations and later acquisition — specifically, while basic coordination skills emerge before age 3, hopping on one leg becomes a typical achievement around age 4. This makes its absence clinically significant when observed beyond normal trajectory.

The agent concludes that absent ability to hop on one leg at age 3 signifies delayed gross motor development due to established guidelines indicating mastery of this skill usually begins at age 4—not within the first three years. Hence, failure to achieve this specific action marks a notable divergence from normative pediatric growth patterns.
